programs.name: Medicaid
programs.tagline: Comprehensive health coverage including nursing home care, assisted living, and home-based care for qualifying seniors.

localEntities.name: Neighborhood Health Plan of RI (NHPRI) for MMP

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ and needs help paying for healthcare or long-term care, Rhode Island Medicaid covers doctor visits, hospital care, prescriptions, nursing home care, and home-based services. Because Medicaid is designed for people with limited income and assets, it includes strict financial limits — but these limits are higher for long-term care services than regular Medicaid.

For long-term care Medicaid (nursing home, assisted living, home care), your parent can have income up to $2,982/month (2026) and still qualify, though they may need to pay a co-share if their income is above certain thresholds. Asset limits also apply, typically around $2,000-$3,000 for singles, but your parent's home and one vehicle are usually exempt.

If your parent already receives Medicare, they may qualify for the Medicare-Medicaid Plan (Neighborhood INTEGRITY) which coordinates all their benefits under one program, eliminating the need to manage separate providers.

summary: Meet nursing facility level of care for LTSS
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment of whether your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or other activities of daily living)

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through HealthSourceRI.com or call 1-855-840-4774, with processing typically taking 45 days.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect proof of income, assets, residency, and medical records if applying for long-term care services.

steps.title: Apply online or by phone
steps.description: Complete application at HealthSourceRI.com or call 1-855-840-4774 for assistance.

steps.title: Complete level of care assessment
steps.description: If applying for nursing home or home care services, schedule clinical assessment to determine if your parent meets nursing facility level of care.

steps.title: Wait for approval
steps.description: Processing typically takes 45 days for aged/disabled applications. You'll receive notice of approval and benefit details.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible for long-term services due to level of care assessment requirements
applicationGuide.tip: SSI recipients automatically qualify for Medicaid — no separate application needed.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Cash value of life insurance over certain limits
excluded: Primary home (if you intend to return and equity is under limits)
Burial funds up to certain amounts

tiers.name: Standard Medicaid
tiers.description: Basic health coverage for adults 65+ with limited income
tiers.coverage: Doctor visits, hospital care, prescriptions, preventive care

tiers.name: Long-Term Care Medicaid
tiers.description: Covers nursing home, assisted living, and home-based care
tiers.coverage: All standard benefits plus nursing home care, assisted living, personal care services, homemaker services

tiers.name: Medicare-Medicaid Plan
tiers.description: Coordinated coverage for people with both Medicare and Medicaid
tiers.coverage: Medicare + Medicaid + long-term services under one plan (Neighborhood INTEGRITY)

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of Rhode Island residency (utility bill, lease)

contentSections.text: Asset limits apply strictly for long-term care services. Home equity may count against limits if over certain thresholds, potentially affecting eligibility.
If your parent's income is above the limit but they need long-term care, they may still qualify but will need to pay a co-share amount. Don't assume they're ineligible based on income alone.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Yes, your parent's primary home is typically exempt from asset limits if they intend to return home. However, home equity limits may apply for long-term care Medicaid, and the state may seek recovery from the estate after death.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over the $2,982 limit?
faqs.answer: He may still qualify for long-term care Medicaid but will need to pay a co-share (his excess income) toward his care costs each month. The program calculates exactly how much he keeps for personal needs.

faqs.question: Does Medicaid cover assisted living or just nursing homes?
faqs.answer: Rhode Island Medicaid covers both nursing home care and assisted living, plus home-based services like personal care and homemaker services. The key is meeting the nursing facility level of care assessment.

faqs.question: If Mom already has Medicare, can she still get Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Yes. If she qualifies for both, she'll likely be enrolled in the Medicare-Medicaid Plan (Neighborhood INTEGRITY) which coordinates all her benefits under one program, making it easier to manage her care.

faqs.question: How long does the application process take?
faqs.answer: Medicaid applications for seniors typically take 45 days to process. If your parent needs long-term care services, add extra time for the clinical assessment to determine their level of care needs.

faqs.question: What happens if we need to spend down assets to qualify?
faqs.answer: Asset spend-down must be done carefully and legally. Strategies exist to help qualify while protecting some assets, but improper transfers can result in penalties. Consider consulting with an elder law attorney for complex situations.

programs.name: Long Term Services & Supports (LTSS)
programs.tagline: If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication, Rhode Island covers care at home or in assisted living.

localEntities.name: Rhode Island Department of Human Services

programs.intro:
If your parent needs daily help with basic activities like bathing, dressing, or taking medication, Rhode Island's LTSS program can cover care services. This Medicaid program pays for home care, assisted living, or nursing home care for parents who qualify financially and medically. Your parent can receive services at home, in assisted living, or in institutional settings — the program covers whatever level of care they need.

To qualify, your parent must need Nursing Facility Level of Care (daily help with basic activities normally requiring nursing home placement) and meet strict financial limits: monthly income under $2,982 and assets under $4,000 for single applicants in 2026. Because the asset limit is extremely low, many families need Medicaid planning strategies before applying.

The program serves Rhode Island residents age 65+ and younger adults with disabilities age 18+. If your parent's income exceeds the limit, they may still qualify but will pay a portion of their income toward care costs.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need Nursing Facility Level of Care — daily help with bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, or medication management that would normally require nursing home placement
otherRequirements: U.S. citizenship or qualifying immigration status

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through HealthSource RI or mail the completed application and supplemental LTSS forms.

steps.title: Complete both applications
steps.description: Fill out the Department of Human Services Application for Assistance AND the supplemental LTSS forms. The basic application alone is not sufficient.

steps.title: Gather financial documents
steps.description: Collect proof of all income sources (Social Security, pensions, IRA payments) and asset documentation. The state reviews past financial transactions, not just current finances.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Apply online at HealthSource RI or mail to Rhode Island Department of Human Services, P.O. Box 8709, Cranston, RI 02920-8787.

steps.title: Complete functional assessment
steps.description: A clinical assessment will evaluate your parent's need for Nursing Facility Level of Care. This measures ability to perform daily activities like bathing, dressing, and medication management.

applicationGuide.tip: Consider consulting a Certified Medicaid Planner before applying if your parent's assets are close to the $4,000 limit — planning strategies may help achieve eligibility.

contentSections.heading: Income: What Counts Toward the $2,982 Limit

offices.name: Rhode Island Department of Human Services

contentSections.text: The $4,000 asset limit is extremely strict. Applying when over the income or asset limits will result in automatic denial. Many families need Medicaid planning before applying.
If your parent's income exceeds $2,982/month, they may still qualify but will need to pay a portion of their income toward care costs (patient share).

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for LTSS?
faqs.answer: The research doesn't specify which assets are exempt from the $4,000 limit. Consult a Certified Medicaid Planner or Elder Law Attorney to understand how home ownership affects eligibility.

faqs.question: What if Dad's Social Security puts him over the $2,982 income limit?
faqs.answer: He may still qualify but will need to pay a portion of his income toward care costs (patient share). The exact amount depends on how much his income exceeds the limit.

faqs.question: Does LTSS cover assisted living costs?
faqs.answer: Yes, LTSS covers services in multiple settings including assisted living, shared living, your parent's home, or nursing homes. The specific services depend on your parent's assessed level of care needs.

faqs.question: How do they determine if Mom needs 'Nursing Facility Level of Care'?
faqs.answer: A clinical assessment evaluates your parent's ability to perform Activities of Daily Living (bathing, dressing, eating, toileting, mobility) and Instrumental Activities (shopping, cooking, cleaning, laundry). They must need daily help with these activities.

faqs.question: What if we're married and only one spouse needs care?
faqs.answer: For married couples where both spouses apply, each is considered individually with the same $2,982/month income limit. The research doesn't specify rules when only one spouse needs care — contact the Department of Human Services for guidance.

faqs.question: How long does the application process take?
faqs.answer: The research doesn't specify processing times. Contact the Rhode Island Department of Human Services directly to understand expected approval timelines for your situation.

programs.name: PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly)
programs.tagline: All medical and daily care coordinated by one team — if your parent needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay home.
programs.intro:
If your parent needs extensive daily help with bathing, dressing, or eating but wants to stay in their home, PACE provides all their medical care through one coordinated team. Your parent gets primary care, specialists, adult day services, transportation to appointments, and home care all managed by the same interdisciplinary team — no coordinating between different doctors or services.

PACE works differently than typical Medicare or Medicaid. Because one team manages everything from routine checkups to emergency care, your parent can't use outside doctors or specialists. But they also won't have copays, deductibles, or coverage gaps. If your parent qualifies for Medicaid long-term services, PACE is free. Private pay averages $4,000–$5,000 per month.

summary: Needs nursing home level of care
Lives in Providence, Woonsocket, or Westerly area
Can live safely at home with support
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need nursing home level of care — extensive daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, toileting, walking, transferring, or eating, as certified by the state
otherRequirements: Must live in PACE service area (excludes Block Island and Prudence Island)
Must agree to receive all care exclusively from PACE providers
Cannot have Medicare Advantage, Part D, or hospice coverage
Must be able to live safely in community with PACE support

applicationGuide.summary: Call PACE RI at 401-434-1400 to schedule an assessment — processing time not specified.

steps.title: Call for assessment
steps.description: Contact PACE RI at 401-434-1400 or enrollment specialist at 401-654-4176 to schedule your parent's functional and medical evaluation.

steps.title: Complete functional assessment
steps.description: A PACE team will evaluate whether your parent needs nursing home level of care and can live safely in the community with support.

steps.title: Provide financial documentation
steps.description: If seeking free coverage through Medicaid, provide income and asset information. Private pay option available without financial review.

steps.title: Choose service center
steps.description: Select Providence, Woonsocket, or Westerly location based on where your parent lives and which area they can access.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Not specified — may vary by service center
applicationGuide.tip: Before applying, confirm your parent is willing to use only PACE providers — they cannot keep their current doctors.

contentSections.heading: Income Limits for Free PACE Coverage (Medicaid)
contentSections.footnote: 2025 Rhode Island Medicaid LTSS limits. Private pay available without income limits.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts for Free Coverage
excluded: Burial funds up to reasonable amount

offices.name: Providence
offices.address: Contact PACE RI for specific location

offices.name: Woonsocket
offices.address: Contact PACE RI for specific location

offices.name: Westerly
offices.address: Contact PACE RI for specific location

categories.name: Medical
items: Recent medical records showing care needs

contentSections.text: Your parent cannot keep their current doctors. PACE requires using only their network of providers for all medical care — primary care, specialists, and emergency services.
Private pay costs $4,000–$5,000 per month, but if your parent's income is over the Medicaid limit, this might still cost less than nursing home care plus coordinating multiple providers.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies for PACE?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary home is completely exempt from PACE asset limits, regardless of value. Your mom can keep her house and still qualify for free PACE coverage through Medicaid.

faqs.question: What if her income is just over the $2,901 limit?
faqs.answer: She can still get PACE services by paying privately — typically $4,000–$5,000 per month. There's no income limit for private pay PACE enrollment.

faqs.question: Can she go to the emergency room at any hospital?
faqs.answer: Emergency care must be coordinated through PACE, but they will arrange and cover emergency services. Your mom cannot independently choose which hospital to use — PACE manages all medical decisions.

faqs.question: What happens if she wants to switch back to regular Medicare later?
faqs.answer: She can disenroll from PACE, but must do so within the first 2 months to avoid being treated as a new applicant if she wants to re-enroll later.

faqs.question: Does PACE provide transportation to the adult day center?
faqs.answer: Yes. PACE includes transportation to and from their day centers, medical appointments, and emergency services as part of the program.

faqs.question: Can she get PACE if she lives on Block Island?
faqs.answer: No. PACE service area covers Rhode Island except Block Island and Prudence Island. She must live within the service area of one of the three centers (Providence, Woonsocket, or Westerly).
